items.title: Hidden Gems: Hunting for Small Boutique Clothing Brands
items.summary: You may have never heard of Fresh Produce, Flax, Spartina 449, M Made in Italy, or Cocco Vino. If not, I am not surprised. Neither had I until I stumbled upon them. These smaller boutique brands fly under the radar of big thrift sites and…
items.content_html:
You may have never heard of Fresh Produce, Flax, Spartina 449, M Made in Italy, or Cocco Vino. If not, I am not surprised. Neither had I until I stumbled upon them. These smaller boutique brands fly under the radar of big thrift sites and mainstream thrift stores. That means you can score incredible deals on genuinely high-quality pieces.

Here is why I love these little-known boutique brands.

Fresh Produce: Breezy, Durable Resort Wear

In 2010, I visited Naples, Florida, and found a cotton skirt on a clearance rack at a beachy boutique. It was breezy, feminine, and surprisingly affordable. I wore it almost every other week that summer and even took it to Italy in 2011. Unfortunately, I left it behind there, and I was devastated.

There is a good reason I mourned that skirt. Fresh Produce specializes in high-quality cotton resort wear. The pieces are casual enough for the boardwalk but polished enough for dinner. In an era when most cotton develops tiny holes from seatbelts or kitchen counters, my Fresh Produce pieces held up far better than my Old Navy ones. The brand runs true to size and is primarily made in the USA. If you find one, snag it.

Flax: Timeless Linen That Lasts

In August 2023, during a family trip, I ducked into a small boutique in Charlottesville, Virginia, while my kids and husband were in a toy store. There, I fell for a beautiful white linen dress from a brand I had never heard of: Flax. It was on sale for $95 (down from $150). I hesitated. My wedding dress did not cost much more. But it ended up being worth every penny!

The fabric was the most durable linen I have ever owned. It flowed beautifully. It worked with sandals or sneakers, and it layered perfectly. I later added a skirt that paired with everything from blouses to tees and even transitioned into fall. After losing some weight, I replaced the dress and skirt with a sleeveless blouse and short-sleeve dress that kept me cool in Japan. If my size stays stable, these pieces will last years.

Flax clothing is generously cut (I am wearing a medium in the photo, although I am normally a L/10-12) and hangs loosely. While the company is headquartered in the USA, their 100% linen items are made in Lithuania.

Spartina 449: Artistic, Nature-Inspired Accessories

I discovered Spartina 449 while hunting for a rattan handbag on Poshmark. Their patterned designs immediately enticed me, especially the ones inspired by nature. Since then, I have added two more bags: one from ThredUp and another gifted by my mom (who is now a convert and owns a beautiful Spartina golf bag).

The brand, headquartered in South Carolina, uses natural materials like linen, cotton canvas, and leather. In-house artists create the patterns, with fabrics produced in Europe before assembly into beautiful handbags, belts, wallets, and more.

M Made in Italy: Elegant Canadian-Italian Designs

I do not own a piece yet, but it is high on my wishlist. M Made in Italy is headquartered in Montreal, Canada, but everything is made in Italy using luxurious natural fabrics like cotton, linen, and silk that drape beautifully. You will find their pieces in boutiques across the US, Canada, and Europe at full retail prices around $60-150. On resale sites like ThredUp, Depop, and Poshmark, though? Total steals. I have got a floral viscose/silk blouse sitting in my wishlist for under $12 right now.

Cocco Vino (or Coco Vino): Italian Boutique Magic

Browsing ThredUp’s “Assorted Brands” section usually yields fast-fashion polyester, but every once in a while, you hit gold. That is how I found a silk-cashmere blend sweater for $24. The fabric feels high-end designer quality.

Cocco Vino is an Italian company that works differently from big brands. Instead of mass production, they collaborate with small Italian textile businesses. Each handles a step like yarn spinning, knitting, or finishing. The result goes to independent boutiques in North America and Europe. Tags might say “Cocco Vino” with European sizing (like 44) or be relabeled “Coco Vino” with North American sizes for some stores. New pieces retail for $100 to $250 depending on the fabric, but thrift finds can be a fraction of that.

Why These Brands Matter (and How You Can Find Them)

Smaller brands like these prioritize quality materials and craftsmanship over trends. They are harder for algorithms to categorize, so savvy shoppers win on resale platforms. Next time you are thrifting online, search beyond the big names. You might just find your new favorite piece that lasts for years.

items.title: Thrift or skip? My honest brand guide
items.summary: When thrifting, designer wins over fast fashion Let me preface this by saying: if you’re thrifting purely for sustainability or tracking down a rare, discontinued piece, buy whatever makes you happy! But if you’re thrifting to save money, you have to buy strategically. Getting the…
items.content_html:
Let me preface this by saying: if you’re thrifting purely for sustainability or tracking down a rare, discontinued piece, buy whatever makes you happy! But if you’re thrifting to save money, you have to buy strategically.

Getting the obvious out of the way... skipping bottom-tier fast fashion like Old Navy, Target's Universal Thread, or Walmart's Time & Tru is a given. These items are almost always cheaper on a retail clearance rack than on secondhand sites. 

If you want to maximize your budget, you have to look closer at mid-tier and luxury labels. Here is my definitive guide to the brands that are actually worth your money secondhand, and the ones you should skip.

Brands to skip

Dealdash / "penny auction" brands:

  • Why? The "penny bidding" website brands like Bolvaint are engineered to look luxury, but carry little value.
  • My experience: I fell victim to this myself. I found a Bolvaint leather bag at a thrift store for $200, complete with a dust bag. It felt like a luxury bag, and a quick phone search showed it retailing for $1,500 online. I thought I hit the jackpot. Once I got home and dug deeper, I realized I’d paid $200 for an unremarkable bag from a site where you can't even buy the items normally.

Diffusion luxury brands:

  • Why? Diffusion lines such as Marc (Marc Jacobs), Michael (Michael Kors), and Lauren (Ralph Lauren) are frequently marked up on thrifted sites, but you can get them a lot cheaper at overstock stores like TJ Maxx or Marshall's.

Fast fashion mall lines:

  • Why? Mall stores, like Gap, Forever 21, Express, and H&M are generally cheaper on sale than thrifting. 
  • My experience: I've had good luck finding cheap Gap denim on ThredUp, but I once bought a thrifted tee for $10, only to realize I could have bought the same item brand new on sale for that exact price.

J. Crew (especially J. Crew Factory) and Banana Republic:

  • Why? Believe it or not, the majority of the J. Crew and Banana Republic items I've seen on thrifted sites are actually marked up from what you can buy them new but on sale at the store. 
  • Caveat: I would definitely thrift higher-end lines like J. Crew Collection or brand collaboration lines like Liberty Fabrics x J. Crew.

Longchamp (especially Le Pliage): 

  • Why? It pains me to say this, because you can often find a good deal on thrifted Longchamp leather bags or rare patterns, but standard Le Pliage bags can be marked up a lot, even more than buying them new on sale.
  • My experience: A Massachusetts thrift shop I visited had a poor condition Le Pliage for $100. I bought my new one on sale at Nordstrom for $125. 

Modcloth:

  • Why? You really have to know what you're looking for here, because Modcloth has changed hands so many times. The original Modcloth designs were unique and high quality, but when the company was acquired by Walmart, then Go Global, they favored cheaper, less quality fabrics. 

Zara:

  • Why? Spain's fast fashion house Zara is really trendy, but the quality is lacking for the thrifting price.
  • My experience: I bought a skirt at Zara for $30 recently, and there's a skirt on ThredUp that looks extremely simiar for $26.99. 

Brands to hunt down

Boden:

  • Why? Even Boden blouses and dresses on sale are rarely as cheap as you can get them on eBay, ThredUp or Poshmark. They're high quality and great for work. 
  • My experience: My closet is filled with Boden clothes I thrifted, and they are still holding up for the price I paid. Wool, linen, and silk are my last forever fabrics.

Cole Haan:

  • Why? Because Cole Haan shoes, even outlet shoes, are super comfortable. You can thrift an excellent condition pair of leather Cole Haan shoes for less than $25, or you can spend $100 on sale at Zappos. Your choice.
  • My experience: I have both outlet versions and boutique versions, and they're still going strong, even after lots of wear.

Everlane:

  • Why?  Even on sale, the popular colors/styles of Everlane jeans are usually upwards of $50, retail over $100. I've never seen them at overstock stores, but thrifted listings are rarely ever over $25.
  • My experience: How do I love Everlane jeans, let me count the ways...especially their "curvy" lines which never gap at the waist (hurray for hourglass and pear shapes!). I have routinely paid under $15 for jeans that last forever.

Farm Rio:

  • Why? Brazilian brand Farm Rio always uses high quality fabrics (you won't find cheap polyester here) and hire in-house artists to do their own fabric designs. If you like their fun patterns and find a thrifted piece for under $40, snatch it up! 
  • My experience: I still haven't been able to find a good price on a Farm Rio dress, either on a thrifted site or on sale at Bloomingdales, for under $70. I'll keep looking.

Luxury brands:

  • Why? High fashion brands like Chanel, Louis Vuitton, Hermes, Celine, Gucci, Fendi, and Chloe rarely go on sale, and rarely are in the affordable price point range. You need to hunt for good deals. I tend to lean on vintage items, but it is possible to find current styles. You can read my blog post about hunting for a cheap Issey Miyake handbag in Japan.
  • My experience: Some of my luxury for less finds:
    • AGL flats ($450 retail, $250 sale, thrifted for $50)
    • Alice + Olivia dress ($400 retail, thrifted for free - I'll write a post about this)
    • Bally vintage handbag ($1000 retail, $800 sale, thrifted for $87)
    • Chloe Paddington bag ($1200 retail, rarely on sale, thrifted for $100)
    • Etro silk blouse (retail $800, $300 sale, thrifted for $17.50)
    • Issey Miyake Bao Bao hand bag ($700 retail, $350 sale, thrifted for $162)
    • M Missoni tank ($450 retail, $300 sale, thrifted for $23) 
    • Paul Green boots ($500 retail, $180 sale, thrifted for $36)
    • Smythe blouse ($350 retail, rarely on sale, thrifted for $35).

Quince (not t-shirts):

  • Why? High quality fabrics (like linen, silk, and cashmere) and good construction make Quince a stand-out contender for thrifting. They only have a sale once a year, and you won't find stellar deals. But even at $80 brand new, a linen dress is a good deal if it lasts for years. You can, however, buy Quince linen or cashmere on Poshmark and not pay more than $20 if you find the right seller.
  • Caveat: I would steer clear from thrifting Quince t-shirts. You can buy them for $16 each or in a 3 pack for $13 each from the website directly - skip the $20 thrift mark-up.

Spring Step L'Artiste:

  • Why? Because Spring Step L'Artiste shoes are beautiful, comfortable, and getting them at a huge retail discount is difficult. Thrifting, however, can pay off if you look hard enough. $15 dress shoes from a local consignment shop sure beats the $120 price on Amazon.
  • My experience: My favorite dress shoes are from this brand, and I was able to score my all-time favorite ankle boots new with tags for under $30. 

Brands I'm on the fence about

Coach:

  • My advice: Only in person, so you can see the inside of the bag.
  • Why? In a brick-and-mortar thrift store, you can score an incredible deal if you know how to spot a boutique bag versus a factory outlet version. Look at the style number inside: if it starts with an F, it’s Coach Factory.

    Online, it’s too much of a gamble. ThredUp often mislabels factory bags as boutique lines, and you can’t inspect the interior photos. The RealReal doesn't distinguish between the two tiers in their listings either, and on Poshmark or eBay, you're relying entirely on the seller's accuracy. Without seeing the interior tag yourself, it's not worth the risk.

Frame: 

  • My advice: Skip unless you know it's a flawless fit or you can try it on.
  • Why? At a $250 retail price, you expect jean perfection. But at a $45 thrift price, the fit and quality can be highly inconsistent. Unless you can try them on first and love the fit, Frame jeans usually aren't worth the secondhand gamble.

Lands' End: 

  • My advice: Proceed with caution. Maybe skip, unless you're looking for a particular piece.
  • Why? Their quality is all over the board. Because Lands' End runs constant retail sales, thrifted prices are rarely worth the gamble unless you know it's a perfect piece.
  • Caveat: If you do gamble on a "New with Tags" piece, buy it on ThredUp instead of retail, solely because ThredUp's return restocking fee is actually cheaper than Lands' End's own return shipping if it doesn't fit!

Luxury-lite brands like Kate Spade or Michael Kors:

  • My advice: Unless you have a dustbag and an original tag from somewhere like Nordstrom, skip.
  • Why? It's tough to determine if the Kate Spade or Michael Kors items on thrifted sites are from the factory outlet or from the boutique store. There are ways to find out, but you really need to do your research. 

Theory:

  • My advice: Probably skip...? 
  • Why? Even though retail prices are high ($350 to $500), I don't know enough about Theory quality to say it's worth the price. On thrift sites, you'll still pay $50 to $80 for basic workwear pieces. Is it worth it? I don't know. You could just buy the Quince lookalikes. I might buy a Theory piece just to be able to comment, they're all over ThredUp.

Vineyard Vines: 

  • My advice: Stick strictly to Vineyard Vines' tried-and-true heritage staples like their fleece or tricot polos. Skip the basic cotton or synthetic blend fast-fashion pieces.
  • Why? The quality is a total hit-or-miss. You might score a gorgeous, high-quality silk blouse, or you might accidentally pay a $40 thrift price for a basic cotton dress that wasn't worth $25 brand new.

In the end...

At the end of the day, labels are just guides, not absolute laws. Brands change their manufacturers, quality fluctuates, and occasionally a "skip" brand might surprise you with a vintage piece that survives the apocalypse.

The bottom line is: you may not know until you get it in your hands, but finding that perfect piece is half the fun!

items.title: Embrace the Wrinkles: Why Linen Is My Go-To Summer Travel Fabric
items.summary: Ready to embrace linen wrinkles? I know what you must be thinking. Over 50… embracing wrinkles… Yes, we all eventually get them on our faces. Believe it or not, this post is not about that! I’m talking about linen and travel. I love linen for…
items.content_html:
I know what you must be thinking. Over 50… embracing wrinkles… Yes, we all eventually get them on our faces. Believe it or not, this post is not about that! I’m talking about linen and travel.

I love linen for summer. In fact, when I go on vacation in the summer, I almost always stick entirely to linen. When we go to Illinois or Tennessee in the summer, it’s really the best thing to wear for that heat and humidity! I love natural fibers that breathe, and I find that as I get older, I want a more polished look and linen definitely has that vibe of “polished, but I am not trying too hard.” Luckily, the majority of hotels and rental houses we’ve been to have irons and ironing boards. 

I love natural fibers that breathe, and as I’ve gotten older, I’ve come to appreciate linen’s polished-but-effortless vibe. Thrifted or Poshmark linen pieces are ideal because they’re often already gently worn-in. When packing for our recent Japan trip, I stocked up on a blouse, pants, and dress, then did my usual due diligence: I checked with hotels and our Airbnb hosts about irons and ironing boards.

Most big chain hotels (like the Hilton Hiroshima) have them. Our Kyoto hostess kindly confirmed she had one. But the Osaka hotel and Tokyo Airbnb? No irons. The Airbnb manager explained they avoid them due to fire hazards. I’d never heard that before, and I stood frowning at my carefully chosen (and very wrinkle-prone) pile of clothes. No iron. What would I do? 

I asked AI for solutions. The usual “hang them in the bathroom during a hot shower” trick came up, along with the surprising note that Japanese people don’t obsess over ironing linen the way Westerners do. So I went to Japan… but I wasn’t fully ready to embrace the wrinkles yet.

I ironed my clothes in Hiroshima. By the time we reached Osaka, I felt confident I could handle rumpled fabric. For those who have never been to Japan, their bathrooms are set up differently than most Western ones. The shower and tub are in a large, fully waterproof room, while the sink and toilet are in a separate area outside. I figured this spacious bathroom setup in the Osaka hotel would be perfect for hanging my linen dress inside while I showered.

Imagine my surprise when the water pressure turned out to be firehose-level strong...not only enough to knock the handheld shower head off its wimpy wall holder, but also to spray around the entire bathroom like a lawn sprinkler. You haven’t lived until you’ve tried drying a dress with a hair dryer in a small hotel bathroom! By the time we got to Kyoto, I was fully prepared to embrace the wrinkles.

What I Learned (and What You Can Use)

Summer fabrics don’t have to be perfectly pressed to look stylish, especially on vacation. Here are a few practical tips:

  • Hang linen or cotton garments in the hotel closet the night before you plan to wear them. Gravity does more work than you’d expect.
  • The shower-steam trick can still help, but test out the shower head before you hang up your clothes in it!
  • Wrinkles show up far less in photos than they do in your head, especially when you’re smiling and making memories.
  • Prioritize breathable natural fibers over perfection.

When I look back at my pictures from Japan, I don’t zero in on the wrinkles in my clothes. I notice how happy we all look and how much fun we were having.

In the end, if you find yourself in a hotel without an iron, don’t stress about it. Pack the linen, go on vacation, and embrace the wrinkles. Are you ready to try it on your next trip?

items.title: 11 packing light tips for international travel
items.summary: Raincoat, umbrella, and a water resistant handbag Let's just be clear - I'm not a pro at this. I did a ton of research to come up with a packing list for my trips to Italy in 2024 and Japan in 2026, and I also…
items.content_html:
Let's just be clear - I'm not a pro at this. I did a ton of research to come up with a packing list for my trips to Italy in 2024 and Japan in 2026, and I also learned a LOT through experience. 

Since we always walk or take public transport when traveling internationally, lugging a massive suitcase is out of the question. We’re so committed to the backpack lifestyle that even on a trip to Arizona where we actually rented a car, we still didn’t bring traditional luggage. There's a unique freedom in skipping the airport check-in lines and completely eliminating the temptation to overpack.

Here are my packing light tips, international or not! (Note - I do not get paid for recommending any of the items mentioned here.)

1. Get a good backpacking pack. And by good, I mean not just durable and roomy, but that is comfortable to wear. A waist strap helps take pressure off your neck and shoulders. 

My backpack is the Osprey Sojourn Porter 65L. I love this pack. It fits everything, it's comfortable to wear, and the thing has about a million pockets. It is expandable, and it has a handle on the side for easy moving when it isn't on your back. I think the nicest feature, though, is that you can unclip the straps and unzip the back, and store all of the straps inside in case you do need to check it. I, however, have flown on 4 different flights with this backpack and was never forced to check it.

My husband has a Gregory Tetrad 75 which we like, but unfortunately it's been discontinued. The main benefit of it, other than being super roomy, is that it has a zip off day pack at the front. Osprey actually makes one that has similar features. We usually pack the Tetrad with everything we don't need on the flight and check it since it's our biggest bag. Then, we use our daypacks as carry-ons to hold the flight essentials, like laptops and charging cords.

I bought the kids both the Deuter Fox 40 for our trip to Italy, they were at the time 9 and 11. They used the same packs on our trip to Japan because of the Deuter Fox's adjustable 'Vari-Quick' strap system. It actually grows with the kids, meaning they can keep using these same bags all the way through middle and high school. My 13 year old is 5'6" right now, and it was perfectly comfortable for him. 

2. Compression bags are game changers. When we went to Italy, we invested in shrink pack travel bags. For the trip to Arizona, in addition to those we bought these Bagsmart compression packing cubes. These save space and keep your clothes organized.

3. Bring less than you think you need. There's no shortage of 'capsule wardrobe' guides online, but unless you're shooting a high-fashion editorial, you can pack way less than they suggest. Even knowing that, I still brought too much on our two-week trip to Japan. Here is everything I brought: 1 sleeveless linen blouse, 1 linen button down blouse, one linen dress, one linen skort, a pair of quick-dry hiking shorts, 1 pair of quick-dry hiking pants, 1 pair of linen pants, 3 t-shirts, a rain coat, 3 camisoles, 2 pajama shorts, 1 sweater, 1 scarf, 5 pairs of socks, and 5 pairs of underwear. I also brought 1 pair of sandals and 1 pair of sneakers. Once we got there, I basically moved into Uniqlo, picking up 3 camisoles, 3 t-shirts, and some quick-dry pants that actually fit. The moral of the story? If you're going to shop at your destination, leave the extra clothes at home. I should have done my homework on how amazing (and affordable) Japanese fashion is.

4. Buy what you need there. Skip packing the daily essentials unless you have specific skin sensitivities or allergies. Items like sunscreen, bug spray, and snacks are actually superior in Japan. As for over-the-counter medications like Sudafed, leave them behind; many countries have strict import rules for pharmaceuticals, so it’s safer to buy local equivalents. Plus, leaving the full-sized liquids at home ensures you won't have to check your backpack at the airport.

5. Bring the lightest water bottles possible. Don't over-pack water bottles. I brought four heavy ones to Italy to leverage Rome’s public fountains, but they quickly became dead weight. Switching to just two lightweight Nalgenes for sharing worked perfectly. Or, better yet: just buy a bottle there and recycle it before you leave.

7. Expandable travel bags are a must! On the trip to Italy, I only brought one, and it was not enough. We had to buy a suitcase. This trip, we brought two. We put all the souvenirs in the backpacks, and all of our dirty clothes in the expandable duffels. I bought this Osprey one recently, and although I don't like the strap, it is an extreme space saver.

8. Research the weather patterns. It sounds like a no-brainer, but I’ve had to buy an overpriced airport umbrella exactly three times because I failed to look ahead. Unless you’re headed to Japan (where brilliant $5 umbrellas are sold at every convenience store), pack a compact travel umbrella if rain is in the forecast.

Knowing we were heading into Japan’s rainy season, I waterproofed our rain jackets and carried my water-resistant, second-hand Tumi handbag, completely skipping any 'just in case' fleeces. Conversely, when we went to Arizona, I packed a hat and gloves for the chilly Grand Canyon winds, but left the 'just in case' raincoat at home. Pack for the reality of the climate, not the hypotheticals.

9. Leave your daily valuables at home. Not only do you not need your Lowe's card in Europe, but if you get pickpocketed (a lesson my mom learned the hard way in Athens), replacing a dozen cards is a logistical nightmare.

Go minimal with your keys, too. I valet park at the airport specifically so I can leave the car keys at the lot and keep the house keys safe at home. It completely eliminates the anxiety of leaning over a railing on a tour and hearing a distant clink.


10. Choose your footwear strategically. For our Italy trip, I only packed one pair of walking sandals and one 'dress up' pair. It was a mistake not to bring a second walking shoe, simply because my feet ache if I don't rotate my footwear. I ended up buying Birkenstocks in Rome just to give my feet a break. Though, for the record, they are so much cheaper in Italian retail stores than in the US!

On the flip side, when we went to Japan, I overcompensated by packing extra shoes for my husband and the kids in case theirs got wet. Total waste of space. Since I had already waterproofed their primary shoes, and they're used to wearing the same pair every day anyway, the extras just took up valuable luggage real estate.

11. Embrace the vacation laundry day. For both our Italy and Japan trips, I intentionally booked an Airbnb with a washing machine right at the halfway mark. To bridge the gaps, I packed TSA-approved laundry soap strips, which are a lifesaver for washing socks and underwear in a hotel sink. Pair those with a universal flat silicone sink stopper, and you never have to worry about a missing hotel plug. This exact setup saved the day in Venice when one of the kids dropped gelato all over his dinner shirt, allowing us to run a quick sink emergency wash instead of carrying a stained shirt for a week.

Ditching the 'just in case' mindset takes a little bit of bravery, but the payoff is instant freedom the second you step off the plane. Skip the baggage carousel, bypass the heavy lifting, and see how much lighter your trip feels when you're living out of a backpack. Your back - and your family - will thank you. Bon voyage!

items.title: What I wore to work (and the Bao Bao story)
items.summary: This bag is THE coolest. I'm probably one of the only people in the world who loves going to work on a Friday. I think it's because I'm naturally an introvert, and the office is silent on Fridays. My colleague and I hang out in…
items.content_html:
I'm probably one of the only people in the world who loves going to work on a Friday. I think it's because I'm naturally an introvert, and the office is silent on Fridays. My colleague and I hang out in a conference room and have fantastic ideas on how to make the technology at our company work better for us. Well, some of the time. A lot of the time we sit on meetings and politely object to things that don't make sense, or explain what it is we plan to do if someone doesn't interrupt us and ask us to fix something immediately broken.

That said, dressing for the office can be daunting, right? Especially in the summer. Not so! Thrifting makes it cheap and easy, and you can bring it all together with a cool handbag. Enter my beautiful Issey Miyake Bao Bao bag I got in Kyoto. There's actually a fun story that goes along with the bag, but first I'll break down my outfit.

The shirt is Lavia 18, which is an off-shoot of the company Piazza Sempione, an Italian company based in Milan. ThredUp find, $12. I'm not super wild about this top to be honest, because it's completely transparent, so I have to wear a tank beneath it. It has nice texture, though, and I feel like that plays nicely with plain linen pants.

The pants are wide leg linen trousers from Quince. I am obsessed with Quince. If you haven't heard of it, go check it out, really nice basics at very high quality for low prices. But how low is low? I bought these pants off Poshmark, new with tags, from a company called (re)vive. I almost don't want to tell people about it to keep it a secret, but they sell overstocks on Poshmark for really cheap. These pants were $10.

The shoes are Ancient Greek Sandals, which I bought on ThredUp for $27. This seemed expensive to me for a thrift price for a company I'd never heard of, until I got the shoes. They are super high quality. I thought, I wonder how much these go for... and looked at their website. Wow! Shoes like these retail for about $400. They're all leather, all handmade in Greece. 

The bag, oh the beloved bag. I have been eyeing Issey Miyake Bao Bao bags for a long time now. They are SO COOL. Lucent Matte retails for about $700 on their website, if you can find it. On ThredUp, they go for between $250-350. I see Nordstrom is selling a shinier one on sale for $500, and Issey Miyake's site has a neat magenta shiny one for $400 on sale. Obviously, I would never spend that on a handbag. So I was determined to find one on the cheap when I went to Japan.

My friend Junko was very kind when told her about my quest for the Bao Bao Lucent tote, and indulged me by letting me drag her all around Honduri Street in Hiroshima looking for a second hand store. I finally on my own went to a shopping mall in Hiroshima, found a second hand designer threads store called Ragtag, and asked if they had one. Yes, they did, in their warehouse, and they shipped it to Kyoto where I was going in 5 days (because he said it takes 3-5 days to ship it to a store from the warehouse). 

I went to the store in Kyoto to pick it up (an adventure, since I went alone). It had some marks on the outside, a "B" rating which means, essentially, it has some wear. The inside was pristine, however. It was 28,000 yen. I said yes, I'll buy it. He actually gave me a discount with my passport to avoid paying the tax, so it was more like 26,000 yen. I didn't know how much that was, but I was thrilled to have my bag. 

The credit card statement says I spent $162 in Ragtag that day. And I was able, with a little dishsoap at the Airbnb, get off the marks. So I basically got a mint condition Bao Bao Lucent for a fraction of the cost. And funny thing is, when I got home, I saw on their global site under "Sold" that the actual bag would have been sold on their site for $250. 

Anyway, that's the story of the bag. Enjoy your weekend, everyone!

items.title: What I wore - June 24, 2026
items.summary: Canva AI put me on a fall street even though it's summer. Nothing says summer like a dress and sneakers, right? (Except instead of showing this awesome outfit in my bedroom, I asked Canva AI to put me on a New England street. But it…
items.content_html:
Nothing says summer like a dress and sneakers, right? (Except instead of showing this awesome outfit in my bedroom, I asked Canva AI to put me on a New England street. But it chose autumn because my prompt wasn’t specific enough, and I ran out of AI credits. Dang it all.) The monochromatic look has really been resonating with me recently.  I’ll break down this look.

The dress: a ThredUp find, Eileen Fisher. It was a severely high-low look, which I actually dislike. I ended up hemming the back so it’s the same length as the front (thank you, sewing machine!). It’s a boat-neck style with cap sleeves, which I dig. The dress is actually a little bit too big on me now, but I can cover up those extra loose armholes with a snazzy white jacket.

The jacket: a local find, new with tags Abercrombie and Fitch from Uptown Consignment, for about $25. This jacket is really quite tailored and high quality. The tag said it was being sold for 100 Euros… which is a little confusing to me. The original owner must have bought it in Europe (I didn’t know A&F had stores in Europe, but Gemini tells me that they have stores in the UK, Germany, and France), brought it home, and never wore it. But that suits me just fine (see what I did there?).

The handbag: a Tory Burch ThredUp find from about 5 years ago, when I first tried ThredUp. This bag was a surprise, actually. It said it was in “Good” condition, but it arrived in a dust bag, with tags on it, and not a scratch to be had. I was thrilled to find it. I always get compliments when I use this bag, it’s so bright and cheerful.

The shoes: these weren’t thrifted, but an Amazon deal, and the most comfortable casual (non-running) sneakers I have. They’re Cole Haan Grand Crosscourt Joy Platform and I bought them for about $75. This is last year’s design, however, so right now they are on sale for $60. I’ve seen these new on Poshmark though, for about $40.

The jewelry: I don’t thrift jewelry enough. I am a big fan of Etsy (I even sell my own jewelry), and I believe I bought the earring studs (you can’t see them, but they are small crosses) on Caitlyn Minimalist, and the necklace I bought 3 years ago on Amazon (you can still buy it today for $35). 

This outfit is polished but super comfortable. I would wear this to my office, or around the house without the jacket (like I am today) and just throw the jacket on if I got chilly.

And that’s my outfit of the day!

items.title: The stinky shirt
items.summary: It's a pretty shirt, am I right? Gorgeous Boden silk-viscose blend blouse, excellent condition, $30. What could go wrong? When I get thrifted clothes online, I always wash them before I wear them. That’s, like, the cardinal rule of thrifting. This situation was no different.
items.content_html:
Gorgeous Boden silk-viscose blend blouse, excellent condition, $30. What could go wrong?

When I get thrifted clothes online, I always wash them before I wear them. That’s, like, the cardinal rule of thrifting. This situation was no different. Silk can be sketchy to wash, but dry cleaning doesn’t always do it for me, and honestly, I’ve never washed anything silk that has disintegrated immediately. The washing instructions said to wash in cold water, so that’s what I did. The shirt had sort of a funky smell, but I figured it would come out in the wash.

I air dried the shirt, then ironed it. OH MY GOODNESS, I’ve never smelled anything like it before. The funky smell turned into the death funk. I nearly passed out. Whoever wore this shirt must have had some serious body odor problem.

So I washed it again, ironed it again, same problem. I let it dry outside in the sunshine. Nothing. I dry cleaned it using Dryel. Still stunk. I tried Febreeze. Nope.

At this point, I was starting to lose hope, so I did two things. 1) I contacted ThredUp and explained the situation over chat. They immediately refunded my money in store credit and told me to keep the item or dispose of it. 2) I logged onto r/ThredUp on Reddit and begged for help.

I got a lot of feedback, almost immediately. It turns out that stinky clothes are something that Redditor thrifters feel really passionately about. Someone recommended soaking in hydrogen peroxide. I tried that, no dice. Someone else said, try vodka. Unfortunately, vodka did not work for me. Although, it was a good use for the vodka I had sitting in my house for ages since I stopped drinking a year and a half ago. Vinegar and baking soda also did not work. Someone else recommended Lysol Sanitizer Detergent. This gave me a little hope, because washing it with the Lysol got out about 40% of the smell. Still not good enough to wear it, but better than before. And now, I wash every new thrifted piece of clothing with it. Lysol do not pay me for this review. You can buy it here.

I was almost about to throw in the towel (ha ha) when someone gave me the most ridiculously complex process which required me buying things I’d never bought before. I joked, I’ve spent about $30 on trying to clean a $30 shirt. But this was a game changer.

Buy Biz Stain and Odor Eliminator Powder. Also buy washing soda (not baking soda). Get a large bowl, put in 1 cup of hot water (not boiling, but hot enough to say “ouch” when you put your finger in it), about 130 degrees F. Dissolve 3 tablespoons of the Biz and the Washing Soda in hot water until it is dissolved. Then add warm water enough to fill the bowl, mix well, and add the shirt. Let it soak for 30 minutes. After 30 minutes, use a spoon to agitate the water. Let it soak for another hour. Then gently squeeze the water out of the shirt, wash it in the washing machine on gentle cycle using a good but gentle detergent like Dreft or Woolite. Air dry.

This worked a treat.

So now, no shirt is too stinky to purchase. Go forth and buy. You’re welcome.

items.title: Beginnings
items.summary: The first step is admitting you have a problem. OK. I have a problem. I love the thrill of the hunt when it comes to thrifting. Many a long night I have laid in bed, after a long day of work, scrolling on my phone…
items.content_html:
The first step is admitting you have a problem.

OK. I have a problem. I love the thrill of the hunt when it comes to thrifting. Many a long night I have laid in bed, after a long day of work, scrolling on my phone through ThredUp or Poshmark, or posting on Reddit asking questions like, “Has anyone else ever purchased a blouse that stunk and if so, how did you get the smell out?” (I’ll make a post about this next!)

The best place to start when you want to figure out how to turn your problem into something good is to start at the beginning, right?

I always loved dressing up. Whether it was in costume, or in fancy dress clothes, there always was something appealing about looking cool. I was six years old, on a trip to visit family in Scotland, and my mother laid out some really fantastic 1980’s sky blue polyester trousers, and I threw a fit, because I wanted to wear a dress. I don’t think my mother relented, as you can see, there exists a photo of me sitting on a cannon outside Edinburgh Castle in those beautifully ugly trousers.

Back to turning my problem into something good. I thought about the fact that I turned 50 about 6 months ago, and how I had no idea how to dress because I spent about 30 years wearing clothes even less fashionable than those sky blue trousers.

I started reading the blog, “40 Plus Style” and it had a lot of great tips, but it also had a lot of expensive clothes. I liked them a lot, but could never afford to spend $160 on a dress from Nordstrom, because inevitably, I’d have to give it away again in 3 months since I was losing weight. I had tried ThredUp in the past, so I thought, maybe I’ll see what they have. It opened up a new world for me.

A year later, I feel like my style has evolved. I’d love to help you, dear reader, on your quest to find your own personal style by buying used!
